title: Error running multiple commands with podman machine ssh on macOS
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I have a lab that uses `podman` and one of the steps is to disable systemd-coredump in the podman machine on macOS. This works using the following 2 commands:

1. `podman machine ssh ln -sf /dev/null /etc/sysctl.d/50-coredump.conf`
2. `podman machine ssh sysctl -w kernel.core_pattern=core`

However, if I try to combine the two commands like so, it fails:

```
$ podman machine ssh sh -c "ln -sf /dev/null /etc/sysctl.d/50-coredump.conf && sysctl -w kernel.core_pattern=core"
Warning: Permanently added '[localhost]:55402' (ED25519) to the list of known hosts.
ln: missing file operand
Try 'ln --help' for more information.
Error: exit status 1
```

When running the `ln` command on its own, it works fine and the exit code is 0:

```
$ podman machine ssh ln -sf /dev/null /etc/sysctl.d/50-coredump.conf
Warning: Permanently added '[localhost]:55402' (ED25519) to the list of known hosts.
$ echo $?
0
```

This makes me think there's a bug in the processing of the `podman machine ssh sh -c` command line arguments.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `podman machine init --rootful=true`
2. `podman machine start`
3. podman machine ssh sh -c "ln -sf /dev/null /etc/sysctl.d/50-coredump.conf && sysctl -w kernel.core_pattern=core"

**Describe the results you received:**

Error in the `ln` command.

**Describe the results you expected:**

The command should work the same whether run standalone or as part of a shell execution.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
% podman version

```

comments.body:
I don't think this is a bug, as this is the behavior and syntax regular ssh does: 

```
$ /usr/bin/ssh -i /Users/acui/.ssh/podman-machine-default -p 53696 core@localhost -o "UserKnownHostsFile /dev/null" -o "StrictHostKeyChecking no" sh -c echo hello; echo hello2

hello2
```

However, doing this command without the `sh -c` works correctly both in `podman machine ssh` and  regular ssh: 

```
$ podman machine ssh echo hello; echo hello2
hello
hello2
```

Feel free to re-open if you think there is more to this.

@ashley-cui 

The point is to run multiple commands in the podman machine using a single `ssh` execution. Your examples are executing one command on the podman machine and the other command on your macOS machine. This point can be more easily explained by changing your `echo` to `uname`:

```
% ssh -i ~/.ssh/podman-machine-default -p 55402 core@localhost -o "UserKnownHostsFile /dev/null" -o "StrictHostKeyChecking no" sh -c uname; uname
Warning: Permanently added '[localhost]:55402' (ED25519) to the list of known hosts.
Linux
Darwin
```

The semicolon after your first example command ends the `ssh` (or `podman ssh`) command and then executes the next command on your machine.

Instead, what my example shows is a failure when running both commands on the `podman machine`; in other words, converting your example would lead to (notice the additional double quotes):

```
% ssh -i ~/.ssh/podman-machine-default -p 55402 core@localhost -o "UserKnownHostsFile /dev/null" -o "StrictHostKeyChecking no" sh -c "uname; uname"
Warning: Permanently added '[localhost]:55402' (ED25519) to the list of known hosts.
Linux
Linux
```

However, as I continued doing some testing, it seems the underlying issue might be a bug in `ssh` itself! I will open a bug report with the `ssh` team. Running on vanilla Linux shows that the direct `ln` command works:

```
$ ssh root@localhost ln -sf /dev/null /etc/sysctl.d/50-coredump.conf
```

But wrapping it doesn't:

```
$ ssh root@localhost sh -c "ln -sf /dev/null /etc/sysctl.d/50-coredump.conf"
ln: missing file operand
Try 'ln --help' for more information.
```

Found the solution. Additional quoting was needed:

```
$ podman machine ssh "sh -c 'ln -sf /dev/null /etc/sysctl.d/50-coredump.conf && sysctl -w kernel.core_pattern=core'"
```

oops! Yep, in my example I forgot quotes as well: `podman machine ssh  "uname; uname"  `

Glad tot see you resolved the issue though!
